prompt: A photorealistic oil painting, hyper-detailed, deeply unsettling. A formal family portrait — the kind hung in the hallway of an old estate. Twelve figures arranged in the classic configuration: elders seated in the front row, adults standing behind, children at the edges. The group, viewed at a distance, reads as warm. Happy. Normal. A family. Together. But every single face, examined individually, tells a completely different story. The grandmother seated at center is smiling — but her eyes are focused on something behind the viewer, something that has made her pupils contract to pinpoints. The grandfather beside her has his mouth open in what reads as a laugh from far away — up close it is a scream caught at the moment before sound arrives. The teenage boy at the left edge appears to be looking at the camera — but his eyes are pointing in two directions, each one focused on something the other cannot see. The small girl at the front appears to be waving — her hand is bent at an angle a wrist cannot make. The standing adults all smile — but no two smiles use the same muscles. Each one is a different approximation of happiness, none of them quite right, as if each face learned the expression from a different and slightly wrong source. The group is a family. Not one of them is what the group suggests. The painting style is Dutch Golden Age portraiture — deep shadow, warm candlelight on faces, meticulous fabric detail, dark background. The canvas appears aged — fine craquelure across the surface, slightly yellowed varnish. The lighting is warm and old. What it illuminates is not. No text, no labels, no symbols. Single frame. Photorealistic oil painting. The horror is not in the darkness — it is in the smiling.
visual_logic: A family portrait reads as warm and normal from a distance. Each individual face, examined closely, is wrong in a different way — the group statistic is true; every member contradicts it.
